[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0036] slide:6:p:2 | A Brief History of Human Factors
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0037] slide:7:p:1 | 7
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0038] slide:7:p:2 | It was during the early 1900s that Frank and Lillian Gilbreth began their work in motion study and shop management around the same time as Frederic Winslow Taylor developed the field of “scientific management”
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0039] slide:7:p:3 | The work of Taylor and the Gilbreths’ can be considered as one of the forerunners to what was later to be called human factors.
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0040] slide:7:p:4 | Their work included
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0041] slide:7:p:5 | study of skilled performance and fatigue
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0042] slide:7:p:6 | and the design of workstations and equipment for the handicapped.
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0043] slide:7:p:7 | 1900’s
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0044] slide:8:p:1 | 8
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0045] slide:8:p:2 | Therbligs
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0046] slide:9:p:1 | 9
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0047] slide:9:p:2 | Prior to W.W.II  much work that was intended to be ergonomics was primarily focused on
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0048] slide:9:p:3 | tests for selecting the proper people for jobs
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0049] slide:9:p:4 | and the development of improved training procedures.
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0050] slide:9:p:5 | “Fit the human to the task” approach
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0051] slide:9:p:6 | Before 1945
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0052] slide:10:p:1 | 10
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0053] slide:10:p:2 | Near the end of the W.W.II in 1945 engineering psychology laboratories were established by the US Air Force and US Navy.
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0054] slide:10:p:3 | This was the result of observing that no matter how much training and job selection went into task performance, the complex equipment still exceeded the capabilities of the people who had to operate it.
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0055] slide:10:p:4 | Thus they were forced to reconsider fitting the equipment to the person.
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0056] slide:10:p:5 | 1945 - 1960
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0057] slide:11:p:1 | 11
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0058] slide:11:p:2 | Fitts developed Fitts' Law, which describes the relationship between task difficulty, movement time, and accuracy
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0059] slide:11:p:3 | Chapanis conducted pioneering research in human factors in aviation and other domains
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0060] slide:11:p:4 | Broadbent applied research on information processing to study attention. In the 1950’s Broadbent used data from behavioral experiments and inferred underlying (cognitive) stages/order of information processing.
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0061] slide:11:p:5 | 1945 – 1960
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0062] slide:12:p:1 | 12
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0063] slide:12:p:2 | Research began to focus on developing optimal design parameters for displays and controls based on understanding of human performance gleaned by Fitts, Chapanis, Broadbent, and many others
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0064] slide:12:p:3 | 1945 – 1960: “knob and dial” era
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0065] slide:13:p:1 | 13
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0066] slide:13:p:2 | Until the 1960’s human factors in the United States was concentrated in the military-industrial related arena.
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0067] slide:13:p:3 | The 20 years between 1960 and 1980 saw rapid growth and expansion of human factors.
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0068] slide:13:p:4 | Two key events lead to the growth of human factors in this era.
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0069] slide:13:p:5 | 1960-1980
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0070] slide:14:p:1 | 14
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0071] slide:15:p:1 | 15
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0072] slide:15:p:2 | The race for space and staffed space flight, human factors quickly became an important part of the space program.
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0073] slide:15:p:3 | Meanwhile, “information processing psychology”, later called cognitive psychology emerged as the primary paradigm to study human cognition
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0074] slide:15:p:4 | Membership in the Human Factors society increased from 500 to 3000 over this 20 year period.
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0075] slide:15:p:5 | 1960-1980
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0076] slide:16:p:1 | 16
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0077] slide:17:p:1 | 17
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0078] slide:17:p:2 | Another event that took place in this period was the Three-Mile Island Incident.
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0079] slide:17:p:3 | This event had a major impact on the perception of a need for human factors in the work place
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0080] slide:17:p:4 | Training: Confused reaction to stress-induced incidents
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0081] slide:17:p:5 | Human-Systems Interaction:
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0082] slide:17:p:6 | Bad instrumentation design and warning systems design
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0083] slide:17:p:7 | Operators did not notice falling pressure
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0084] slide:17:p:8 | Too much irrelevant and misleading information
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0085] slide:17:p:9 | 1960-1980
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0086] slide:18:p:1 | 18
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0087] slide:18:p:2 | Human Factors has continued to be an area of rapid growth
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0088] slide:18:p:3 | Ergonomics needs are vastly changing with automation and the information age
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0089] slide:18:p:4 | Introduction of Human Computer Interaction: Focus is changing from physical modeling to cognitive and communication modeling
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0090] slide:18:p:5 | Cognitive Science emerges in this period, an interdisciplinary field drawing from diverse fields beyond only psychology, including but not limited to linguistics, computer science, philosophy, and neuroscience
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0091] slide:18:p:6 | 1980 - Beyond

[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0093] slide:19:p:2 | A Brief Review of Relevant Human Factors Concepts
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0094] slide:20:p:1 | ‘Anthro’ & ‘Metric’
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0095] slide:20:p:2 | Anthro = Relating to humans
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0096] slide:20:p:3 | Metric = Measurement or to measure
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0097] slide:20:p:4 | Anthropometric = Measurement of humans
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0098] slide:20:p:5 | Measurements are typically physical in nature.
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0099] slide:20:p:6 | Like height, weight, volume, distance between two landmarks on the body, and strength capabilities.
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0100] slide:20:p:7 | However, sensory abilities may also be measured: such as hearing ability, sight, and the ability to sense touch.
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0101] slide:20:p:8 | Anthropometrics
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0102] slide:21:p:1 | The process or result of becoming aware of objects, relationships, and events by means of the senses, which includes such activities as recognizing, observing, and discriminating.

[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0206] notes:7:p:1 | In the early 1990s Gilberths found that the key to improving work efficiency was in reducing unnecessary motions. Not only were some motions unnecessary, but they caused employee fatigue and increased opportunity for unsafe events to occur.
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0207] notes:7:p:2 | The work of the Gilbreths is often associated with that of Frederick Winslow Taylor, yet there was a substantial philosophical difference between the Gilbreths and Taylor. The symbol of Taylorism was the stopwatch; Taylor was concerned primarily with reducing process times. The Gilbreths, in contrast, sought to make processes more efficient by reducing the motions involved. They saw their approach as more concerned with workers' welfare than Taylor, which is often perceived as concerned mainly with profit.

[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0228] notes:9:p:1 | Towards the end of WW2, the military started to realize that no matter how much training and job selection went into task performance, the complex equipment still exceeded the capabilities of the people who had to operate it. And, so they were forced to reconsider fitting the equipment to the person.
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0229] notes:10:p:1 | Research in this period laid the foundation for understanding human movement and response time as well as the relationship between human performance and system design. Broadbent’s work is especially notable because it occurred at a time when Behaviorism dominated psychology departments. The idea of studying unobservable aspects of the human ‘system’ was novel and challenged the status quo. Given that fact, some have suggested that Broadbent was the first cognitive psychology.
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0230] notes:12:p:1 | With the advent of human space flight, human factors took off. (pun intended).

Look at bright light, and you close your eyes, you still see white spots for a few seconds.
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0284] notes:33:p:3 | Sensory memory is an ultra-short-term memory and decays or degrades very quickly, typically in the region of 200 - 500 milliseconds (1/5 - 1/2 second) after the perception of an item, and certainly less than a second (although echoic memory is now thought to last a little longer, up to perhaps three or four seconds).
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0285] notes:33:p:4 | Information is passed from the sensory memory into short-term memory via the process of attention (the cognitive process of selectively concentrating on one aspect of the environment while ignoring other things), which effectively filters the stimuli to only those which are of interest at any given time.

It can be thought of as the ability to remember and process information at the same time. It holds a small amount of information (typically around 7 items or even less) in mind in an active, readily-available state for a short period of time (typically from 10 to 15 seconds, or sometimes up to a minute).
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0288] notes:33:p:7 | Long-term memory is, obviously enough, intended for storage of information over a long period of time. Despite our everyday impressions of forgetting, it seems likely that long-term memory actually decays very little over time, and can store a almost unlimited amount of information almost indefinitely.
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0289] notes:33:p:8 | There is actually  some debate as to whether we actually ever “forget” anything at all, or whether it just becomes increasingly difficult to access or retrieve certain items from memory.

[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0291] notes:34:p:1 | Basic Definition: At its core, risk refers to the possibility of an adverse event occurring and the uncertainty about its outcome.
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0292] notes:34:p:2 | Everyday Examples:
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0293] notes:34:p:3 | Academic: Choosing to not study for a major exam poses a risk of failing.
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0294] notes:34:p:4 | Financial: Spending your savings on a spring break trip might mean you won't have money for textbooks next semester.
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0295] notes:34:p:5 | Social: Going to a party the night before an early class can risk your attendance or attentiveness in that class.
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0296] notes:34:p:6 | Distinguish between Risk and Uncertainty:
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0297] notes:34:p:7 | Risk can be measured and quantified. For example, if you've skipped 50% of your classes, you can statistically analyze your risk of failing based on past patterns.
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0298] notes:34:p:8 | Uncertainty, on the other hand, involves situations where outcomes are unpredictable. For example, even if you've attended all your classes and studied hard, there's still some uncertainty about what questions will appear on your final exam.
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0299] notes:34:p:9 | Risk-Reward Tradeoff: Often, greater risks come with the possibility of greater rewards. But higher potential rewards might not always be worth the risks.
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0300] notes:34:p:10 | Career: Pursuing a start-up right after college might offer big potential rewards but also has a high risk compared to a stable entry-level job.
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0301] notes:34:p:11 | Investments: Stocks might offer higher returns than bonds, but they also come with higher volatility.
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0302] notes:34:p:12 | Risk Management: Just because risks exist doesn't mean they should always be avoided. Instead, they should be managed.
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0303] notes:34:p:13 | Prioritization: Focus on studying for an exam worth 50% of your grade versus one worth 5%.
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0304] notes:34:p:14 | Diversification: Instead of putting all your summer earnings into one stock, consider spreading it out over multiple investments.
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0305] notes:34:p:15 | Insurance: Like how health insurance can help cover unexpected medical bills, there are ways to hedge or insure against certain risks.
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0306] notes:34:p:16 | Personal Risk Tolerance: Different people have different appetites for risk based on their personality, life experiences, and current circumstances. Some may be more willing to take on debt to pursue a graduate degree, believing in the future payoff, while others prefer a more conservative financial approach.
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0307] notes:35:p:1 | 39

[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0311] notes:37:p:2 | If two tasks compete for attentional resources i.e., involve same cognitive process(es), high performance in one task means low performance in another task
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0312] notes:37:p:3 | Driving while texting
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0313] notes:37:p:4 | Multitasking can cause people to lose their train of thought, make errors, and need to start over
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0314] notes:37:p:5 | If two tasks are independent i.e., involve completely different cognitive process(es), performance in one task has no influence on the other
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0315] notes:37:p:6 | E.g. reading while eating a snack
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0316] notes:37:p:7 | Driving is very demanding
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0317] notes:37:p:8 | Reaction times are longer to external events when talking on the phone in a car (Caird et al., 2018)
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0318] notes:37:p:9 | Phone drivers rely more on their expectations about what is likely to happen next as conversation taking up their attention
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0319] notes:37:p:10 | Response time is slower to unexpected events (Briggs et al., 2018)
[01-intro-to-hf-and-risk-1-0320] notes:37:p:11 | Drivers often try to imagine what the other person’s face is like – whom they are talking to

docs.title: ISO 14971 and Medical Device Risk Management
docs.text:
This page treats ISO 14971 not as a single worksheet, but as the language and process framework for risk management across the medical-device lifecycle.

Core Question

How does ISO 14971 organize hazard, hazardous situation, harm, control, and post-market feedback into a closed-loop risk-management process?

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• The point of ISO 14971 is not paperwork; it is a consistent language for managing risk.
  • hazard, hazardous situation, and harm must stay clearly separated.
  • Risk management is not a one-time activity but a continuous process of planning, identification, control, verification, and post-market review.
  • Human-factors work matters here because many hazardous situations arise through use.

docs.title: What This Method Is For
docs.text:
Start with the Purpose

Remember the role of the standard this way: it does not replace design work; it forces the team to place design risk, use risk, and harm consequence inside one closed loop.

docs.title: What Problem This Method Solves
docs.text:
ISO 14971 solves the problem of teams talking about risk imprecisely. It forces a shared language that separates sources of harm, exposure situations, and actual harm so design, clinical, regulatory, and human-factors discussions can connect.

The Most Common Failure Mode

The most common error is collapsing hazard, hazardous situation, and harm into the same layer. Once those three are blurred, later controls and verification logic drift with them.

docs.title: What the Inputs and Outputs Are
docs.text:
Inputs include design information, intended use, user scenarios, hazard identification, testing information, and post-market feedback.

Outputs include at least:

  • a risk-management plan
  • risk-analysis entries
  • control and residual-risk judgments
  • a mechanism for feeding post-market information back into the system

docs.title: How the Method Proceeds
docs.text:
The process can be remembered in six steps:

  1. establish the risk-management plan
  2. identify hazards and foreseeable use situations
  3. estimate and evaluate risk
  4. implement risk controls
  5. judge residual risk and overall acceptability
  6. feed production and post-market information back into the process

One-Sentence Conclusion

The strength of ISO 14971 is that it keeps the team using one shared risk structure instead of letting each function describe risk in its own language.

Worked Example: How to Write a Syringe Risk Chain Clearly

If a dose window is hard to read, the hazard may be the drug-delivery capability itself, the hazardous situation is the user misreading the dose and delivering too much, and the harm is the patient’s adverse physiological outcome. Once those layers are separated, controls can be assigned correctly to display design, confirmation workflow, and validation testing.

docs.title: How It Connects to Human Factors
docs.text: Human factors is not an external add-on to the standard. Many hazardous situations appear through use error, interface-induced confusion, labeling issues, and task-context limits, which is why the later medical-device URRA and use-error pages continue the same line.

docs.title: Why Risk Control in This Standard Is Not “Add Whatever Control Comes to Mind”
docs.text:
ISO 14971 does not mainly reward the team for listing many risks. It rewards the team for thinking clearly about control logic. One recurring course judgment is that if a hazard can be reduced through design itself, the analysis should not start by relying on labeling or training.

That creates a control hierarchy:

  • redesign the source of risk where possible
  • then add protective measures or system defenses
  • only then depend on information, instructions, training, or alerts

The reason is not that the later methods never help. It is that source-level controls are usually more stable and less dependent on perfect user behavior under pressure.

Example: Why “It Is Clearly Stated in the IFU” Usually Cannot Be the Primary Control

If a high-risk misuse can already be reduced through display design, physical constraint, interface separation, or forced confirmation, then writing the primary control as “warn the user in the IFU” is usually weak. This does not mean the IFU is useless; it means the standard prefers stable controls over handing risk back to reading and memory.

docs.title: Why the Standard’s Language Has to Be So Strict
docs.text:
Many people see ISO 14971 for the first time and feel the definitions are overly fine-grained. That precision is exactly why the standard is useful. Once the team blurs hazard, hazardous situation, and harm, three problems appear quickly:

  • controls no longer target the right layer
  • verification no longer knows what it is supposed to demonstrate
  • residual-risk discussion turns into several departments speaking past each other

The language rule therefore looks like terminology discipline, but in practice it protects the causal structure of the analysis from collapsing.

docs.title: Why Probability Levels Help Discussion but Cannot Be Treated as Absolute Truth
docs.text:
Probability labels such as Frequent, Probable, Occasional, Remote, and Improbable mainly help the team develop a shared ranking language. They do not mean every risk can be estimated with high precision.

That is especially true for use-related risk, where scenario differences, user variation, and control strength can shift the picture substantially. The more mature practice is to use the scale as a communication scaffold and then read it together with severity, detectability, and control reliability instead of staring at the label alone.

This figure should make one point visible: probability levels are valuable because they standardize ranking language, not because they magically turn every risk into a precise calculation.

docs.title: Why ISO 14971 Brings the Full Lifecycle into Scope from the Start
docs.text:
This page is not limited to development-time risk. It explicitly places manufacturing, storage, transport, use, service, and disposal inside scope. That scope itself expresses a judgment: patient risk is not generated only at the moment of use. Many hazardous conditions are planted earlier in manufacturing, distribution, or maintenance.

That is also why risk management cannot belong to the design team alone. Once lifecycle is included, quality, manufacturing, service, post-market surveillance, and human factors all have to connect inside the same loop.

docs.title: Why the Standard Ultimately Has to Connect to the QMS and Design Controls
docs.text:
The real strength of ISO 14971 is not only that it provides vocabulary. It embeds risk judgment into the quality system. Risk management therefore cannot remain an isolated document. It has to influence design input, design review, verification, validation, and post-market CAPA.

That is also why the course mentions it alongside ISO 13485, 62366, and software standards. The standards are not listed as parallel trivia. They signal that risk language must flow into the quality-management system, otherwise risk analysis stays on paper.

docs.title: Why Post-Market Information Is Not Merely an End-of-Process Add-On
docs.text:
Many teams treat post-market information as the epilogue that begins only after launch, but ISO 14971 places it inside the loop to emphasize something else: real-world complaints, service data, misuse patterns, and field issues are supposed to revise the earlier risk judgments.

In other words, this is not the final chapter. It is the start of the next cycle. Once the field shows a new failure mode, a new user population, or a new environmental pressure, the earlier hazard, control, and residual-risk judgments should be revisited.

Example: Why One Post-Market Complaint Can Force the Team to Revisit Earlier Residual-Risk Judgments

If field complaints repeatedly show that users struggle to read the dose window after transport exposure, that is not merely a service issue. It signals that earlier assumptions about readability, storage and transport condition, and residual-risk acceptability may have been too optimistic. The ISO 14971 loop expects the team to send that real-world information back into the risk chain rather than leaving it in a customer-support database.

docs.title: Chapter Summary
docs.text:
What To Carry Forward

  • ISO 14971 provides a common risk language and a closed-loop process.
  • hazard, hazardous situation, and harm must remain distinct.
  • Risk management spans planning, control, verification, and post-market feedback.
  • Human factors is not peripheral inside the medical-device risk chain.

[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0059] slide:10:p:2 | Key risk management activities from ISO 14971:

[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0065] slide:10:p:8 | Risk management review
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0066] slide:10:p:9 | Production and post-production activities

[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0069] slide:11:p:2 | Key interactions between the RM process defined in ISO 14971 and the Usability Engineering process from IEC 62366-1
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0070] slide:12:p:1 | Definitions
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0071] slide:12:p:2 | Benefit: positive impact or desirable outcome of the use of a medical device on the health of an individual, or a positive impact on patient management or public health
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0072] slide:12:p:3 | Harm: Injury or damage to the health of people, or damage to property or the environment
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0073] slide:12:p:4 | Hazard: Potential source of harm
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0074] slide:12:p:5 | Hazardous situation: Circumstance in which people, property, or the environment is/are exposed to one or more hazards
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0075] slide:12:p:6 | Intended use: Intended purpose; use for which a product, process or service is intended according to the specifications, instructions, and information provided by the manufacturer
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0076] slide:12:p:7 | Life cycle: Series of all phases in the life of a medical device, from the initial conception to final decommissioning and disposal
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0077] slide:12:p:8 | Reasonably foreseeable misuse: use of a product or system in a way not intended by the manufacturer but which can result from readily predictable human behavior
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0078] slide:12:p:9 | Residual risk: risk remaining after risk control measures have been implemented
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0079] slide:12:p:10 | Risk: combination of the probability of occurrence of harm and the severity of that harm
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0080] slide:12:p:11 | Risk analysis: Systematic use of available information to identify hazards and to estimate the risk
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0081] slide:12:p:12 | Risk assessment: Overall process comprising a risk analysis and a risk evaluation
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0082] slide:13:p:1 | Definitions - continued
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0083] slide:13:p:2 | Risk control: Process in which decisions are made and measures implemented by which risks are reduced to, or maintained within, specified levels
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0084] slide:13:p:3 | Risk estimation: Process used to assign values to the probability of occurrence of harm and the severity of that harm
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0085] slide:13:p:4 | Risk evaluation: Process of comparing the estimated risk against given risk criteria to determine the acceptability of the risk
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0086] slide:13:p:5 | Risk Management File (RMF): Set of records and other documents that are produced by risk management
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0087] slide:13:p:6 | Safety: Freedom from unacceptable risk
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0088] slide:13:p:7 | Severity: Measure of the possible consequences of a hazard
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0089] slide:13:p:8 | Use error: User action or lack of user action while using a medical device that leads to a different result than intended by the manufacturer or expected by the user
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0090] slide:13:p:9 | Verification: Confirmation, through the provision of objective evidence, that specified requirements have been fulfilled
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0091] slide:14:p:1 | Risk Analysis
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0092] slide:14:p:2 | From ISO 14971:2019 Figure C.1
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0093] slide:14:p:3 | Risk analysis: Systematic use of available information to identify hazards and to estimate the risk
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0094] slide:15:p:1 | Risk Analysis
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0095] slide:15:p:2 | Risk Analysis is the process used to identify hazards and to estimate risk. It includes identifying:
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0096] slide:15:p:3 | The intended use, intended users, and intended use environment of the device*
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0097] slide:15:p:4 | Reasonably foreseeable misuses of the device*
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0098] slide:15:p:5 | The characteristics of the device that could impact safety
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0099] slide:15:p:6 | The Hazards associated with design, manufacturing, and use of the device
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0100] slide:15:p:7 | Hazardous situations that could arise from the hazards (i.e. how people, property, the environment can be exposed to a hazard)
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0101] slide:15:p:8 | The resulting harms (i.e. consequences of the exposure to the hazardous situation)
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0102] slide:15:p:9 | Estimating the risk (i.e., the severity of the harm the probability of the harm occurring)
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0103] slide:15:p:10 | * Intended use, users and use environment as well as foreseeable misuses are inputs to the risk analysis process from the “Use Specification” - an output of the Usability Engineering process
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0104] slide:16:p:1 | Risk Analysis: Key Concepts
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0105] slide:16:p:2 | All risk analysis is based on the device’s intended use, intended users, and intended use environment and must include “reasonably foreseeable misuses” of the device and consider risks arising in in normal and fault conditions
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0106] slide:16:p:3 | Hazards can be associated with design (including software), manufacturing, and use of the device as well as its distribution, storage and servicing
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0107] slide:16:p:4 | A hazardous situation occurs when people, property or the environment are exposed to a potential source of harm (hazard) through a sequence of events. (Not all hazards lead to hazardous situations)
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0108] slide:16:p:5 | An injury (harm) can only occur after exposure to the hazard
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0109] slide:16:p:6 | A hazardous situation may lead to different harms
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0110] slide:16:p:7 | Clinical expertise is necessary to identify harms and estimate the consequences of the harms (severity)
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0111] slide:16:p:8 | Risk analysis starts early during product development and continues throughout the product’s life cycle (iterative)
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0112] slide:16:p:9 | From ISO 14971:2019 Figure C.1
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0113] slide:17:p:1 | Risk Analysis – Sources of hazards
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0114] slide:17:p:2 | From ISO 14971:2019 Table C.1
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0115] slide:17:p:3 | ISO 14971 has a list of potential hazards and examples of hazardous situations
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0116] slide:17:p:4 | Relevant product safety standards (e.g., IEC 60601-1) will also list hazards
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0117] slide:17:p:5 | IEC 62366-1 Annex B provides examples of hazardous situations related to usability
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0118] slide:17:p:6 | Data from similar types of devices/designs should also be evaluated during hazard identification
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0119] slide:18:p:1 | Examples of hazards, SoEs, hazardous situations and harms
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0120] slide:18:p:2 | From ISO 14971:2019 Table C.3
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0121] slide:18:p:3 | From IEC 62366-1:2015+A1:2020 Table B.2
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0122] slide:19:p:1 | Risk Analysis - Risk Estimation
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0123] slide:19:p:2 | Risk estimation is the process used to assign values to the probability of occurrence of harm and the severity of that harm.
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0124] slide:19:p:3 | Severity
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0125] slide:19:p:4 | Severity of harm is a continuum that will depend on numerous factors (such as the level of exposure to the hazardous situation, a patient’s pre-existing conditions and co-morbidities)
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0126] slide:19:p:5 | To simply risk analysis a few discrete levels are generally used to categorize severity using common terms and descriptors appropriate for the medical device
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0127] slide:19:p:6 | Each harm is assigned a severity level which is determined using clinical/medical expertise and can be based on the reasonable worst-case consequence
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0128] slide:19:p:7 | The severity rating does not include probability
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0129] slide:19:p:8 | Examples of common Severity terms and descriptions from ISO 24971:2020 Table 4
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0130] slide:20:p:1 | Risk Analysis - Risk Estimation
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0131] slide:20:p:2 | Probability of Harm
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0132] slide:20:p:3 | The probability of harm can be either qualitative or quantitative. Qualitative estimates should be used for new products
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0133] slide:20:p:4 | Like severity, probability is also a continuum and discrete levels are generally used to categorize it
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0134] slide:20:p:5 | The probability of harm is sometimes broken into two parts – the probability of the hazardous situation occurring (P1) and the probability that the harm of specified severity occurs when the hazardous situation has occurred (P2)
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0135] slide:20:p:6 | Examples of common probability terms and descriptions from ISO 24971
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0136] slide:20:p:7 | P1 includes elements of the sequence of events that lead to the exposure to the hazard. The individuals assigned to estimate the probability P1 should be familiar with the design, construction, use and servicing of the medical device, as well as have an adequate understanding of the use environment.
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0137] slide:21:p:1 | Risk Analysis - Risk Estimation
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0138] slide:21:p:2 | The P2 estimate will include a clinical aspect thus the individuals assigned to estimate P2 should be familiar with the medical use of the device
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0139] slide:21:p:3 | P2 is often estimated using expert clinical judgment and experience with similar devices, informed by adverse event data, medical literature and information from post-production.
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0140] slide:21:p:4 | Detailed understanding of the performance of the medical device is generally not needed to estimate P2
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0141] slide:21:p:5 | Combining the severity of harm and the probability of harm produces the risk estimate

[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0204] slide:21:p:68 | S3P1
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0205] slide:21:p:69 | Low Risk

[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0210] slide:21:p:74 | A risk matrix is often used to show the combination of the severity level and the probability of harm level (S x Ph)
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0211] slide:21:p:75 | Each Severity x Probability of harm combination is assigned a risk level (e.g., Low, Medium, High) that is then used to evaluate the risk
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0212] slide:22:p:1 | Risk Evaluation
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0213] slide:22:p:2 | Risk evaluation: Process of comparing the estimated risk against given risk criteria to determine the acceptability of the risk
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0214] slide:22:p:3 | Risk evaluation is used to determine if risk control is required to reduce a risk to an acceptable level
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0215] slide:23:p:1 | Risk Evaluation
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0216] slide:23:p:2 | The criteria used to determine the acceptability of individual risks is defined prior to starting risk analysis
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0217] slide:23:p:3 | ISO 14971 does not specify risk acceptability criteria since what is acceptable differs by culture, regulatory agencies and even at an individual level. Thus, each medical device manufacturer is required to define its own risk acceptability criteria
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0218] slide:23:p:4 | Risk Level
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0219] slide:23:p:5 | Risk Acceptability
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0220] slide:23:p:6 | Definition
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0221] slide:23:p:7 | Low Risk
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0222] slide:23:p:8 | Acceptable
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0223] slide:23:p:9 | Residual risk is acceptable and additional risk reduction/risk control measures are not required.
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0224] slide:23:p:10 | Medium Risk
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0225] slide:23:p:11 | Broadly Acceptable
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0226] slide:23:p:12 | Residual risk is broadly acceptable. However, risk reduction should be investigated. Application of “state of the art” risk control measures such as meeting safety requirements identified in applicable standards shall be considered to reduce risk to an acceptable level.
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0227] slide:23:p:13 | High Risk
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0228] slide:23:p:14 | Unacceptable
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0229] slide:23:p:15 | Residual risk is unacceptable and additional risk control measures shall be implemented. If implementing additional risk control measures is not possible a benefit risk analysis must be performed to ensure that the clinical/medical benefits outweigh the residual risk.
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0230] slide:23:p:16 | Device manufacturers often use the risk levels determined during risk estimation as a starting point for risk acceptability.
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0231] slide:23:p:17 | Low risks are often deemed acceptable without risk reduction being required
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0232] slide:23:p:18 | Medium and high risks will require additional action to reduce the risk
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0233] slide:23:p:19 | Common risk acceptance criteria include:
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0234] slide:23:p:20 | Risk must be reduced “As far as possible” considering generally accepted state of the art technology
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0235] slide:23:p:21 | Requirements from relevant safety standards have been implemented
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0236] slide:24:p:1 | Risk Control
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0237] slide:24:p:2 | Risk control: Process in which decisions are made and measures implemented by which risks are reduced to, or maintained within, specified levels.
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0238] slide:25:p:1 | Risk Control
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0239] slide:25:p:2 | Risk Control is a process step in ISO 14971 that includes:
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0240] slide:25:p:3 | Risk control option analysis
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0241] slide:25:p:4 | Risk control measure (RCM) implementation
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0242] slide:25:p:5 | Residual risk evaluation
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0243] slide:25:p:6 | Benefit-risk analysis (for unacceptable residual risks)
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0244] slide:25:p:7 | Identifying and analyzing risks arising from risk control measures
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0245] slide:25:p:8 | Checking completeness of risk control
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0246] slide:25:p:9 | All risks that do not fall into an acceptable range must be reduced
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0247] slide:25:p:10 | Risk Control Measures (RCM) are used to reduce risks
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0248] slide:26:p:1 | Risk Control – Key Concepts
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0249] slide:26:p:2 | All risks that do not fall into an acceptable range must be reduced
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0250] slide:26:p:3 | If Risk Reduction is not possible, a benefit-risk analysis must be performed to ensure that the clinical benefits outweigh the risk
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0251] slide:26:p:4 | Risk Control Measures (RCM) are used to reduce risks
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0252] slide:26:p:5 | The term “risk control” is often used interchangeably for risk control measures and the ISO 14971 risk control process step
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0253] slide:26:p:6 | A main focus of device RM should be on reducing identified risks - not in risk estimation
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0254] slide:26:p:7 | ISO 14971 lists three types/categories of risk control measures:
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0255] slide:26:p:8 | inherently safe design and manufacture
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0256] slide:26:p:9 | protective measures in the medical device itself or in the manufacturing process
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0257] slide:26:p:10 | information for safety and, where appropriate, training to user
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0258] slide:26:p:11 | Priority should be given to inherent safety risk control measures followed by protective measures and finally information for safety
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0259] slide:26:p:12 | Multiple risk control measures may be required to reduce a risk to an acceptable level
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0260] slide:26:p:13 | Testing is not considered a risk control measure under ISO 14971
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0261] slide:26:p:14 | All RCMs must be implemented and verified to be effective in reducing risk
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0262] slide:27:p:1 | Risk Control
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0263] slide:27:p:2 | Risk Control Measure types/categories:
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0264] slide:27:p:3 | Examples from ISO 24971
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0265] slide:28:p:1 | Risk Control
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0266] slide:28:p:2 | Examples of Inherent Safety RCMs:
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0267] slide:28:p:3 | Description
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0268] slide:28:p:4 | Example
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0269] slide:28:p:5 | - eliminating a particular hazard
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0270] slide:28:p:6 | Eliminating the hazard of sharp edges that can cause injury by designing the surfaces with rounded edges.
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0271] slide:28:p:7 | Eliminating the hazard of electric shock by using a manually operated pump instead of an electrical pump.
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0272] slide:28:p:8 | - reducing the probability of occurrence of the harm
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0273] slide:28:p:9 | Reducing the probability of fibrillation harm due to an electric shock by having no accessible live parts. Reducing the probability of unauthorized access to data by identity management.
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0274] slide:28:p:10 | Reducing the probability of biological reactions due to microbial contamination by using cleanroom technologies or sterilization.
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0275] slide:28:p:11 | - reducing the severity of the harm
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0276] slide:28:p:12 | Reducing the severity of harm from being squeezed by a moving part by using a low-power motor and low speed.
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0277] slide:28:p:13 | Reducing the severity of harm from an electric shock by using low electric voltage (below 42 V).
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0278] slide:28:p:14 | Examples of Inherent Safety RCMs for use-related hazards:
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0279] slide:28:p:15 | Option
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0280] slide:28:p:16 | Examples
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0281] slide:28:p:17 | Inherent safety by design
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0282] slide:28:p:18 | • Use specific connectors that cannot be connected to the wrong component.
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0283] slide:28:p:19 | • Remove features that can be mistakenly selected or eliminate an interaction when it could lead to use error.
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0284] slide:28:p:20 | • Improve the detectability or readability of controls, labels, and displays.
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0285] slide:28:p:21 | • Automate device functions that are prone to use error when users perform the task manually.
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0286] slide:28:p:22 | Examples from ISO 24971
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0287] slide:28:p:23 | Examples from IEC 62366-1
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0288] slide:29:p:1 | Risk Control
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0289] slide:29:p:2 | Examples of Protective Measure RCMs:
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0290] slide:29:p:3 | Examples of Protective Measure RCMs for use-related hazards:
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0291] slide:29:p:4 | Description
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0292] slide:29:p:5 | Example
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0293] slide:29:p:6 | - preventing the occurrence of a hazardous situation
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0294] slide:29:p:7 | Using automatic cut-off or over-pressure valves.
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0295] slide:29:p:8 | Protective covers of electrical wires and power units (covered plugs, sockets and connectors).
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0296] slide:29:p:9 | Guards for moving parts or to prevent patients falling off a table or out of bed. Inspection testing in manufacturing to detect non-conforming products.
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0297] slide:29:p:10 | - preventing a hazardous situation from leading to harm
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0298] slide:29:p:11 | Using visual or acoustic alarms to alert the user to a hazardous situation.
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0299] slide:29:p:12 | Option
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0300] slide:29:p:13 | Examples
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0301] slide:29:p:14 | Protective measures in the medical device itself or in the manufacturing process
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0302] slide:29:p:15 | • Incorporate safety mechanisms such as physical safety guards, shielded elements, or software or hardware interlocks.
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0303] slide:29:p:16 | • Include warning screens to advise the user of essential conditions that should exist prior to proceeding with device use, such as specific data entry.
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0304] slide:29:p:17 | • Use alerts for hazardous conditions, such as a “low battery” alert when an unexpected loss of the device’s operation could cause harm or death.
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0305] slide:29:p:18 | • Use device technologies that require less maintenance or are “maintenance free.”
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0306] slide:29:p:19 | Examples from ISO 24971
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0307] slide:29:p:20 | Examples from IEC 62366-1
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0308] slide:30:p:1 | Risk Control
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0309] slide:30:p:2 | Examples of Information for Safety RCMs:
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0310] slide:30:p:3 | Risk Control Option
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0311] slide:30:p:4 | Example
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0312] slide:30:p:5 | - placing warnings on the medical device;
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0313] slide:30:p:6 | Warning: Do not use after [expiry date].
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0314] slide:30:p:7 | - including contra-indications in the accompanying documentation;
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0315] slide:30:p:8 | Do not use with neonates.
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0316] slide:30:p:9 | - providing instructions to support correct use and to avoid use error;
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0317] slide:30:p:10 | Apply epinephrine injector to the middle of your outer thigh (upper leg), through clothing if necessary. Do not inject into your veins, buttocks, fingers, toes, hands or feet. Hold the leg of young children firmly in place before and during injection to prevent injuries.
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0318] slide:30:p:11 | - providing instructions to use personal protective equipment;
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0319] slide:30:p:12 | Use gloves and eyeglasses when handling toxic or hazardous materials.
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0320] slide:30:p:13 | - providing instructions about measures to reduce the severity of harm;
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0321] slide:30:p:14 | Rinse immediately with water after contamination with hazardous substances.
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0322] slide:30:p:15 | - providing training to users on how to use the medical device correctly;
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0323] slide:30:p:16 | Training program for operators of radiotherapy equipment or for home-use dialysis machines. Note: Operators may be required to certify to training completion.
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0324] slide:30:p:17 | - providing instructions relating to installation and maintenance during the lifetime of the medical device
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0325] slide:30:p:18 | Maintenance intervals, maximum expected lifetime, how to dispose of the medical device properly.
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0326] slide:31:p:1 | Risk Control
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0327] slide:31:p:2 | Examples of RCM options for various devices:
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0328] slide:31:p:3 | Examples from ISO 24971
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0329] slide:31:p:4 | Examples from IEC 62366-1
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0330] slide:32:p:1 | Risk Control - Verification
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0331] slide:32:p:2 | Verification of implementation and effectiveness of the risk control measures:
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0332] slide:32:p:3 | Objective evidence must be provided to show that all risk control measures have been implemented and that the RCMs effectively reduce risk
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0333] slide:32:p:4 | Examples of RCM verification of implementation may include design documentation, design specifications, manufacturing process documentation and process qualification
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0334] slide:32:p:5 | Examples of verification of RCM effectiveness may include design verification, design validation – including usability validation, and manufacturing process validation
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0335] slide:33:p:1 | Risk Control - Verification
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0336] slide:33:p:2 | Verification of implementation and effectiveness examples
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0337] slide:33:p:3 | Examples from ISO 24971
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0338] slide:34:p:1 | Risk Control - Residual Risk Evaluation
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0339] slide:34:p:2 | Residual risk is the risk remaining after risk control measures have been implemented and verified to be effective
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0340] slide:34:p:3 | The risk is re-estimated and re-evaluated using the same risk estimation and risk evaluation process described in detail above
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0341] slide:34:p:4 | If a residual risk is not judged acceptable using the defined acceptability criteria, further risk control measures must be considered
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0342] slide:34:p:5 | If further risk control is not practicable for an unacceptable risk, a benefit-risk analysis must be performed to determine if the benefits of the intended use outweigh the residual risk
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0343] slide:34:p:6 | If the benefits do not outweigh the residual risk, then the manufacturer may consider modifying the medical device or its intended use. Otherwise, the risk remains unacceptable
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0344] slide:35:p:1 | Evaluation of Overall Residual Risk
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0345] slide:35:p:2 | After all risk control measures have been implemented and verified, the manufacturer shall evaluate the overall residual risk posed by the medical device, taking into account the contributions of all residual risks, in relation to the benefits of the intended use, using the method and the criteria for acceptability of the overall residual risk defined in the risk management plan
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0346] slide:36:p:1 | Evaluation of Overall Residual Risk (vs. Benefit)
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0347] slide:36:p:2 | The evaluation of overall residual risk is the point where residual risk is viewed from a broad perspective
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0348] slide:36:p:3 | Even though all individual risks have been reduced to an acceptable level (or have been accepted based on benefit-risk analysis) from the risk control process step, the overall device risk must be evaluated against the benefit the device provides:
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0349] slide:36:p:4 | Benefit: positive impact or desirable outcome of the use of a medical device on the health of an individual, or a positive impact on patient management or public health
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0350] slide:36:p:5 | This step is particularly important for complex medical devices and for medical devices with many individual risks. The evaluation can lead to the conclusion that the medical device is safe and effective – but only if its benefits outweigh its risks
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0351] slide:36:p:6 | The overall residual risk must be evaluated by persons with the knowledge, experience and authority to perform such tasks. Ultimately, the evaluation should be based on expert judgment with essential roles for application knowledge and clinical expertise
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0352] slide:36:p:7 | Only when a device’s clinical benefit exceeds its overall residual risk can it be commercialized
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0353] slide:37:p:1 | Device Risk Management Key Takeaways
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0354] slide:37:p:2 | ISO 14971 provides the framework for successful medical device risk management. Use it and other applicable standards as a baseline for developing safe and effective devices
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0355] slide:37:p:3 | Safe and effective for their intended use with their intended users in their intended use environment
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0356] slide:37:p:4 | All devices have some level of risk. The benefits of using a device must outweigh its risks
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0357] slide:37:p:5 | Risk analysis and Risk control are key activities for successfully managing device risks
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0358] slide:37:p:6 | Look to standards and guidance documents as well as readily available real-world data on your device for sources of hazards, hazardous situations, and harms
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0359] slide:37:p:7 | Involve clinical and medical specialists when assessing harms and severities
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0360] slide:38:p:1 | Q & A
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0361] notes:1:p:1 | Quick introductions
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0362] notes:2:p:1 | All devices will have some level of risk. What is “Acceptable” varies from culture to culture and person to person. Device risk has to be considered in the context of the benefits it provides weighed against the risks. The broad definition of “Unacceptable risk” is if the benefits a device provides does not outweigh its risks
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0363] notes:2:p:2 | Definition of Risk: the combination of the probability of occurrence of harm and the severity of that harm
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0364] notes:2:p:3 | 2
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0365] notes:3:p:1 | Goal is to present a high-level overview of some of the key elements of medical device RM based on ISO 14971 – internationally recognized device RM standard
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0366] notes:3:p:2 | 3
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0367] notes:4:p:1 | How does ISO 14971 fit into the regulatory framework? Standards are voluntary but in general can help implement the intent of the regulations
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0368] notes:4:p:2 | Note: level of detail generally decreases moving up the triangle. Companies use standards and guidance to inform their policies and procedure to meet the requirements of the regulations and laws.
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0369] notes:4:p:3 | 5
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0370] notes:5:p:1 | There are many medical device standards that should be used to help develop safe and effective products. I’ve included a few for reference. Note specifically IEC 62366-1 for the usability engineering process.
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0371] notes:5:p:2 | 6
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0372] notes:6:p:1 | Note the difference between process standards and product safety standards. Process standards define requirements for a process while product safety standards identify hazards/risks and requirements for controlling those risks.
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0373] notes:6:p:2 | 7
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0374] notes:7:p:1 | Most standards are voluntary (at least for FDA). However, FDA and other regulatory bodies “recognize” certain standards as “state of the art” and being able to show conformance to a recognized standard reduces regulatory burden for companies. Product safety standards are good sources of hazards and possible controls. Same with FDA guidance documents.
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0375] notes:7:p:2 | 8
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0376] notes:8:p:1 | Note that 14971 provides high level requirements for RM but does not specify implementation. It leaves the actual implementation up to medical device manufacturers
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0377] notes:8:p:2 | 9
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0378] notes:9:p:1 | 14971 is a process standard – defines a standardized RM process framework. Steps in the process are defined in the flow chart. Note that process steps include inputs and generate outputs used in other steps. Also note that RM is iterative and outputs from the process are used in other QMS processes like design controls and human factors (and vice versa). Focus on Risk Analysis, Risk Evaluation, Risk Control, Evaluation of Overall Residual Risk
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0379] notes:9:p:2 | 10
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0380] notes:10:p:1 | Usability (and use error) is a key component of medical device risk management. This diagram taken from 62366-1 shows the interactions between the usability engineering process and the risk management process define in ISO 14971
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0381] notes:10:p:2 | 11
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0382] notes:11:p:1 | Definitions from ISO 14971 have been included for reference. Key concepts for analyzing risk are Intended use, Hazard, Hazardous situation and Harm.
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0383] notes:11:p:2 | 12
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0384] notes:12:p:1 | This is the process flow diagram from before. Discuss Risk analysis first. Defined as the Systematic use of available information to identify hazards and to estimate the risk
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0385] notes:12:p:2 | 14
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0386] notes:13:p:1 | Risk analysis is the “Systematic use of available information to identify hazards and to estimate the risk”
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0387] notes:13:p:2 | Risk analysis includes several required steps listed here. The first two bullets are inputs to the RA process - generally taken directly from the usability engineering process. Refer to the 14971 – 62366-1 interaction diagram
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0388] notes:13:p:3 | 15
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0389] notes:14:p:1 | Emphasize: “reasonably foreseeable misuse” and “normal and fault conditions”
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0390] notes:14:p:2 | and sources of risks – traditionally design, manufacturing and use
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0391] notes:14:p:3 | 16
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0392] notes:15:p:1 | Hazard identification is a key step in Risk analysis. Standards are a good source of examples of hazards and hazardous situations. Annex C in 14971 lists categories that many companies use a “checklist” when identifying hazards to be analyzed based on a specific device
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0393] notes:15:p:2 | 17
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0394] notes:16:p:1 | Here are some examples taken from 14971 and 62366. The analysis starts with identification of a hazard – e.g., high voltage, identifies a hazardous situation, the possible harms and a foreseeable sequence of events that could expose the patient to the hazard.
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0395] notes:16:p:2 | After Hazards have been identified, Sequences of Events, Hazardous situations and potential harms have been identified the next step in Risk Analysis is to estimate the risk.
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0396] notes:16:p:3 | 18
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0397] notes:17:p:1 | Recall that risk consists of two parts – severity of harm and the probability that the harm (of a certain severity) occurs. Harm severity and probability are evaluated separately
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0398] notes:17:p:2 | 19
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0399] notes:18:p:1 | Qualitative vs Quantitative: quantitative should only be used when statistically significant probability data exists
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0400] notes:18:p:2 | 20
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0401] notes:19:p:1 | Note: the P1 x P2 approach is not required. This shows an example of a risk matrix and how it’s often used for risk evaluation in the device industry.  However, the approach is not required by 14971 although 24971 shows an example. Each manufacturer has to define its own risk estimation process and scales.
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0402] notes:19:p:2 | 21
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0403] notes:20:p:1 | After risk has been estimated it must be evaluated. Risk evaluation is used to determine if risk control is required to reduce a risk to an acceptable level, i.e., it’s input to the risk control process step
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0404] notes:20:p:2 | 22
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0405] notes:21:p:1 | This is a commonly used approach but is not in 14971. Each device company has to define its own risk evaluation process and risk acceptability. 24971 provides some examples.
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0406] notes:21:p:2 | 23
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0407] notes:22:p:1 | 24
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0408] notes:23:p:1 | 26
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0409] notes:24:p:1 | Definitions of what each type of control might entail. More specific examples to follow
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0410] notes:24:p:2 | 27
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0411] notes:25:p:1 | These are included for reference. Again, standards and TIRs are good sources for controls
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0412] notes:25:p:2 | 28
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0413] notes:26:p:1 | 29
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0414] notes:27:p:1 | 30
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0415] notes:28:p:1 | Examples for reference
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0416] notes:28:p:2 | 31
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0417] notes:29:p:1 | After risk control measures have been identified they are implemented in the design or manufacturing process and have to be verified to show that they are effective in reducing risk. Note: Design V&V is sometimes used to show VoE but these are different concepts
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0418] notes:29:p:2 | 32
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0419] notes:30:p:1 | 33
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0420] notes:31:p:1 | This is an iterative process
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0421] notes:31:p:2 | 34
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0422] notes:32:p:1 | One more key step – Overall Residual Risk acceptability
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0423] notes:32:p:2 | 35
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0424] notes:33:p:1 | Requires weighing the totality of risks against the benefits of using the device
[iso-14971-and-medical-device-rm-0425] notes:33:p:2 | 36

docs.title: Known Problem Analysis and Event Trees
docs.text:
This page pairs two gap-finding methods: Known Problem Analysis looks backward to historical issues, while Event Tree analysis looks forward from an initiating event into downstream consequence branches.

Core Question

Once the main workflow analysis is complete, how does the team continue asking what historical issues and downstream consequence branches may still be missing?

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Known Problem Analysis prevents the team from rediscovering old failure modes too late.
  • Event Trees prevent the team from underestimating the downstream branches of an initiating event.
  • Together they expand the view from the main path to edge paths and missed risk.
  • These methods are not administrative extras; they are deliberate blind-spot checks.

docs.title: What This Method Is For
docs.text:
Start with the Purpose

Remember the role of these methods this way: main workflow analysis clarifies the main path, while Known Problem Analysis and Event Trees protect against forgetting the edges and branches of the system.

docs.title: What Each Method Adds
docs.text:
Known Problem Analysis captures what the team may already know through complaints, incidents, CAPA records, literature, or competitor history.

Event Tree expands what happens after an initiating event: if one barrier fails, what branches follow, and which nodes function as real defenses?

The Most Common Failure Mode

The most common misunderstanding is to treat known problems as an old-issue list and event trees as a drawing exercise. Their real function is to change the team’s sensitivity to missed risk.

docs.title: How the Methods Proceed
docs.text:
The two methods can be remembered separately:

  1. Known Problem: scan historical problems first, then decide which still apply to the current design.
  2. Event Tree: define the initiating event first, then expand the downstream branches, intercept points, and consequence severity.

Neither method is about free-form brainstorming. Both force the team back toward evidence and foreseeable pathways.

Worked Example: The Main Path Looks Fine, but the Edge Path Still Fails

Suppose the normal use flow of a device is well analyzed, but complaint history shows users often reverse a component after cleaning and reassembly. Main-path analysis may miss that low-frequency branch. Known Problem Analysis pulls it back into view, and Event Tree analysis then asks what happens if that reversed assembly is not detected.

docs.title: How They Relate to the Main Workflow Analysis
docs.text:
Main workflow analysis is strongest on the normal task path. These two methods are strongest at exposing historical blind spots and branch consequences outside that main path. They complement rather than replace the main analysis.

One-Sentence Conclusion

The real value of this page is that it forces the team to step back from “we already analyzed it” and ask “what might still be missing?”

docs.title: Why Known Problem Analysis Also Has to Learn Signal Selection
docs.text:
Known problems come from many places, but not every historical signal carries the same weight. A more mature practice is learning how to separate scattered noise from repeated signals that point to the same structural weakness across complaints, recalls, literature, and competitor events.

This page therefore teaches more than where to look. It also trains you to compress historical material into actionable risk cues rather than accumulating sources without analytic direction.

docs.title: Why Known Problem Analysis Prevents the Team from Repeating Old Failures
docs.text:
Many system risks are not novel. They have already appeared as complaints, CAPA records, accidents, literature findings, or competitor failures. Known Problem Analysis matters because it forces the team to recognize that history is itself risk evidence; every project does not begin from zero.

When done well, this step makes the team notice problems that are not new, but are still likely to recur in the present environment.

This figure should show that known problem analysis is not casual memory. It requires going into real databases such as MAUDE to identify recurring device problems and complaint patterns.

docs.title: Why Event Trees Pull Side-Branch Risk Back into View
docs.text:
Main workflow analysis often stays focused on the most common path, but many serious system outcomes emerge on the branches. The value of an Event Tree is that it begins with one initiating event and keeps asking:

  • what happens if this defense succeeds
  • what happens if it fails
  • which outcomes are rare but extremely costly

Event Trees therefore do more than predict consequences. They force the team to reinterpret what each defense branch actually means.

Example: Why “An Alarm Occurs” Is Not the End but the Start of an Event Tree

If the initiating event is “a critical alarm occurs,” the real analysis begins afterward: was it noticed, was it understood, was the correct action taken, did the system continue degrading, and did backup defenses engage? Once expanded this way, the team sees that the real risk is not the alarm itself but the whole chain of later defense branches.

docs.title: Why KPA Is Not Just “Finding Cases” but a Three-Step Filtering Process
docs.text:
The lecture explicitly breaks KPA into select sources -> collect and analyze -> document findings to show that this is not a contest in gathering material. Strong KPA first decides which sources matter, then judges which issues are actually relevant to the current product, interface, and use conditions, and only then translates the useful findings into risk and mitigation clues.

That sequence matters because without source and similarity filtering, teams quickly drown in historical material. The core of KPA is not merely knowing that many bad things happened before. It is recognizing which old problems still have a realistic chance to recur in the current project.

Example: Why an Old Blood-Glucose-Meter Problem Cannot Stop at “Copying a Complaint Summary”

If KPA only copies an old complaint into documentation, the team still does not know why it matters for the current design. A stronger KPA continues by asking whether the current strip-insertion method, prompt timing, blood-drop icon placement, and action rhythm could still induce the same failure. Only then does the historical issue become a design input for the present project rather than background reading.

docs.title: Why the Point of an Event Tree Is Not Drawing a Tree but Seeing Where Defenses Break
docs.text:
Many people first encounter Event Trees as branching diagrams and focus on the diagram itself. What the course really trains is something else: once you move forward from the initiating event, where were the opportunities to block escalation, and how did those opportunities fail one by one.

In that sense, the value of an Event Tree is not only consequence branching. It is that every branch becomes a statement about whether a defense succeeded or failed. Once read that way, the tree stops being only an outcome picture and becomes a structured inventory of recovery opportunity.

docs.title: Why This Page Is Especially Good at Recovering Low-Frequency High-Consequence Paths Missed by Main Workflow Analysis
docs.text:
Main workflow analysis is usually strongest on the most common and stable path, yet many of the most costly accident chains live on edge branches. Known Problem Analysis reminds the team whether those branches have already surfaced historically, and Event Trees then ask how the consequence would amplify if they happened again.

The most useful lesson of this page is therefore that low frequency does not mean low importance. Many branches are rare, but still deserve serious design, validation, and training attention.

docs.title: Chapter Summary
docs.text:
What To Carry Forward

  • Known Problem Analysis looks backward to prior issues; Event Trees look forward to consequence branches.
  • Together they fill gaps rather than replace main workflow analysis.
  • Both methods force the team back to evidence and foreseeable paths.
  • The true target is discovering what the team still missed.
